Thank you for your request for advice on the conflict of 
interests provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
The facts, as 
presented to me, are that a recall election is going to be held 
Involving four Clearlake councilmembers. 
You are the City Clerk 
and you want to make and participate in the decisions related to 
this recall election. 
The City Attorney indicated that you 
should dlsqualify yourself from any involvement because one of 
tne changes hoped to be aChieved by tne recall election is a 
redefinition of the position of City Clerk, witn a corresponding 
increase in salary. 
you have asked whether your participation 
in the recall decisions will involve a conflict of interest. 
The Political Reform Act prevents a public official from 
making, or participating in the making of, a governmental 
decision in whicn he Knows, or has reason to know, he has a 
financial interest. 
(Government Code Section 87100.11 
An 
official nas a "financial interest" ln a decision within the 
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseable that the 
decision will materially affect a source of income aggregating 
$250 or more received by the public official within 12 months 
prior to the time wnen tne aecision is made. 
Section 82030(b) (2) excludes from the definition of "income" 
salary received from a local government agency. 
Thus, the

Dear Mr. Leidigh: 
Pursuant to our telephone conversation, I am requesting advice 
on a conflict of interest issue under the provisions of 
Government Code Section 83ll4(b). 
I have enclosed the following for your review: 
1. 
3/18/83 memo from City Attorney to City Clerk (re-
garding conflict of interest). 
2. 
Newspaper articles concerning possible recall and 
interest in reorganization of City Government (not 
a complete file - further information may be ob-
tained from the Clear Lake Observer). 
3. 
Notice of Intent to Recall filed 3/8/83. 
4. 
3/21/83 letter of advice from Elections Division 
of Secretary of State's Office (regarding conduct 
of recall). 
5. 
2/28/83 memo from City Attorney (regarding conduct 
of recall). 
6. 
3/18/83 memo from City Attorney to City Council 
(regarding services of County Clerk). 
7. 
My 3/3 
83 letter to Clearlake Observer (I do not 
know if the letter has been or will be published.) 
The original Notices of Intent filed 3/8/83 failed to contain

the provisions of Elections Code Section 27023 and the pub-
lication was also incomplete. 
New Notices of Intent to Re-
call four councilmen were filed yesterday with my Deputy. 
Two sets of Notices of Intent were filed on 4/5/83 -- one 
with the same proponents (for a "by district" recall) and 
another set with entirely different individuals (for an "at 
large" recall). 
All of the notices contained the exact word-
ing as the original notices for the reasons for recall. 
As you will see, there are conflicting opinions as to the 
manner in which the recall proceedings should be conducted 
at this time -- at large or by district. 
Mr. Thunen, in his 
3/18/83 memo to the City Council, states that the County 
Clerk is prepared to verify signatures on recall petitions. 
This statement is not entirely accurate. 
The County Clerk 
has advised me that she does not have the voter registrations 
divided into our City Districts and would be unable at this 
time to verify signatures if Districts 2 and 4 had to be re-
called in that manner. 
At present the City Clerk of Clearlake receives $150.00 per 
month salary as set by Resolution of The City Council. 
She 
is to perform all duties outlined in the California Codes 
with the exception of accounting duties which have been trans-
ferred to an appointed Director of Finance. 
In addition she 
acts as secretary to the City Council. 
I feel there is no conflict of interest in performing my 
duties as City Clerk in accordance with Elections Code sec-
tions relating to recall proceedings. 
Please advise if, in your opinion, there is sufficient reason 
to declare a conflict of interest in this matter. 
I would 
also appreciate a legal interpretation of my responsibility 
for the actions of my Deputy City Clerk in the event I am 
found in conflict of interest and she is requested to make the 
necessary decisions on the recall proceedings. 
Thank you for your cooperation and an early response.

CONFLICT OF INTEREST; PENDING RECALL PROCEEDINGS 
In view of the fact that considerable publicity has been given 
to the position of the Clearlake Citizens' Committee that one 
of the changes in municipal government which they hope to d-
chieve by means of the pending recall is redefinition of the 
job of the City Clerk and a corresponding increase in the sal-
ary for that position, I conclude that Government Code Section 
87100 
prohibits you from making any decision relating to the 
validity of the recall proceedings at any stage of the pro-
ceeding. 
Government Code Section 87103 defines financial interest as 
including any source of income of $250.00 or more which was 
promised within twelve months prior to the time when the de-
cision is to be made. 
The case of Witt vs. Morrow, 70 Cal. Ap. 
3rd 817 (1977) indicates that it is not necessary to prove that 
the decision of the official will have a significant impact on 
the financial interest, but rather only that it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect. 
It must be stated that it is now reasonably foresee-
able that a successful recall election will have a material 
effect on your compensation as City Clerk over the balance of 
your four year term. 
I am mindful of the fact that Government Code Section 87101 
provides that Section 87100 does not apply where there is no 
one else to make the decision. 
However, it is also true that 
Elections Code Section 22003 permits the governing body of any 
city to request the Board of Supervisors of the county to per-
mit the County Clerk to render specified services to the city 
relating to the conduct of an election. 
I have contacted the 
County Clerk, who has stated that she is willing to perform 
the task of verifying signatures on the various recall peti-
tions. 
Further, pursuant to Ordinance No.9, Section 3, and

Government Code Section 7, you may delegate any task re-
lated to this recall to your deputy, who does not appear 
to have any financial interest in the outcome of the pend-
ing proceedings. 
If you have any questions regarding this opinion, please 
do not hesitate to contact me.

Format of a city recall election 
ballot in addition to the name of the 
officer requires tbequestion, "If tbe 
recall prevails shall the (name- of 
legislative body, such as Clearlake 
City Council) fill the vacancy or 
vacancies by appointment or call a 
special election for that purpose? 
Following the question the words 
"by appointment" and "by special 
election" shall appear on separate 
lines. 
If less than a majority of the 
council (one or two members) is 
recalled the seats are immediately 
vacated and the remainder of the 
council 
would 
appoint 
the 
replacement if the majority of the 
voters favored this method or the 
remainder of the council would call 
an election to fill the vacancies. 
The election must be called not 
less than 88 nor more than 103 days 
after the date of the order calling for 
the election. If a regular municipal 
election is to occur not more than 
118 nor less than 88 days from the 
date of canvassing the vote (on the 
recall petition) the council may 
provide for filling the vacancy at the 
regular municipal election instead of 
at a special election. The next regular 
municipal election in Clearlake will 
take place the first 
Tuesday in 
April, 1984. 
The recalled officer may not be a 
candidate to succeed himself orber-
self at a special election held to fill 
the vacancy created by his or her 
recall nor may he or she be appoin-
ted by the legislative body to fill the 
vacancy. 
If a majority of the council is 
recalled at the same election (three or 
more) the members recalled shall 
retain their offices until their suc-
cessors are elected anp qualified. 
. Immediately after the canvass of 
votes the clerk shall call a special 
election to fill the vacancies arising 
as a result of the recall election to be 
held within 89 days after canvass of 
votes of the recall election has taken 
place. 
In the event of filling vacancies 
created 
by 
recall 
by 
election 
nominations of candidates shall be 
made in the same way as in a regular 
election. Nomination papers and 
declaration of candidacy shall, in 
each case, be filed no less than 59 
days prior to the date of the election 
and not before the day the order of 
the election is issued. 
For a recall to be effective one half 
or more of the votes cast must favor 
the recall.

eee chairman defends recall 
CLEARLAKE -
Teresa Rifesi, 
chairman pro-tem of the Clearlake 
Citizens Committee, told members of 
the Newcomer's Club Monday noon 
that a new city council could turn 
things around giving the city gover-
nment that would produce more 
direct benefits for the people of the 
city rather than for a select group of 
employees. 
Rifesi gave a brief review of the 
committee's organization stating that 
its purpose has been to serve the 
council by giving it 
information 
regarding what the people in the city 
of Clearlake wanted. "Things didn't 
turn out quite that way," she com-
mented. 
The former Clearlake city clerk 
said the committee now numbers in 
the hundreds; however, total mem-
bership and names of members are 
not divulged, she said, in order to 
protect them from possible reprisals. 
She defended 
the committee's 
recall effort against four city coun-
cilmen, Mayor Jack Lucich, Gene 
Achter, Dr. Hewlett and Ed Robey, 
on the grounds that while recall is 
not an attractive way to go it is the 
only recourse the people have. 
"We (committee members) were 
told by the city attorney that the 
people had nothing to say about the 
way the city was being run. This was 
a shock, indeed. We were told we 
had nothing to say and that our only 
recourse was recall. We couldn't 
believe this, so the directors jour-
neyed to Sacramento to find out 
where the people stood only to learn 
there that recall was the only path 
open to them. 
"Recall doesn't 
deserve 
the 
reputation it has because it is the 
vehicle provided by law whereby the 
people can assert their will. It gives 
the citizens the opportunity to decide 
if they like what is going on or not." 
Rifesi added that 
recall was 
something she had personally always 
abhorred, but under present circum-
stances she likened it to an oppor-
tunity to make changes if the 
majority of the people concur. 
She brought out 
the present 
allocation of money by the council as 
one major cause for the recall. She 
maintained that a conservative coun-
cil could redo the city budget making 
more money available for vitally 
needed services, particularly in the 
public works department. She was 
critical of the generous salaries being 
paid to department heads. As an 
example she cited the position of 
building inspector who has no staff 
under him who receives $25,320 per 
year plus a recently granted 3.2 per-
cent increase. The similar position in 
Lakeport pays $18,000 and the in-
dividual oversees three other em-
ployees and has been there a number 
of years. The Clearlake building of-
ficial received his training at city ex-
pense and was a former employee of 
PMI with less than twO year's ex-
perience, she said. 
Rifesi supports a city government 
with an elected city clerk in charge of 
the clerical functions of the city 
stating this would be less costly than 
the present set-up and the clerk 
would be directly responsible to the 
people as an elected official. 
When asked about the cost of the 
recall, an estimated $7,000 for each 
of two elections if two are needed, 
she said in her view the new council 
could effect savings that would more 
than offset this cost. 
"We are not seeing the benefits we 
expected from incorporation," Rifesi 
averred. She was a member of the 
Incorporation Committee. "I had 
seen when working for the county 
the 
small 
amount 
of money 
generated in 
the Clearlake com-
munity that was returned to this area 
and therefore I was a strong suppor-
ter of incorporation. Never in my 
wildest dreams could I have foreseen 
what we now have in the form of 
city government," she declared. 
She assured the audience that the 
recall was not a personal attack on 
any of the men involved but that 
they were instrumental in delegating 
absolute authority to their appointed 
city manager and for allocating fun-
ds in a manner that brought tOO little 
direct benefit to the people. 
Earlier in her talk she declared 
that the committee has never set it-
Teresa Rifesi. 
self up as representing all of the 
people of Clearlake. She said 
this would be a mistake and the 
committee has never taken such a 
position. 
She then referred to the telephone 
surveys undertaken among city 
registered voters. "We contacted at 
least 20 percent of the registered 
voters and the results were alarming. 
Eighty percent of those called said 
they were dissatisfied 
with 
the 
present city government. She said 
that calls to more than 200 commit-
tee members also revealed a majority 
in favor of recall." 
Rifesi, in conclusion, said "If you 
the people vote the recall down then 
that is wonderful, that is what the 
majority of you want." She said that 
no one on the committee's board of 
directors is aspiring to public office 
but noted that there were many 
qualified people in town who would 
make good candidates if the recall 
were successful.

Your letter of February 25 raised the question of whether a city 
council member may be recalled by voters of the member's district 
or of the entire city, where the member had been elected by district, 
but where at the time of that election the city's voters had 
determined to se ect council members at large in future elections. 
The question appears not to have been decided by statute or the 
courts to date. 
After reviewing applicable statutes and case law 
governing election of municipal officers and recall, we have 
concluded that courts faced with the question would most likely 
decide that such council members must be recalled by district. 
Government Code Section 34870 and following sections authorize 
voters of cities to determine to select council members by or from 
districts, and to amend that determination. 
Under the tatutory 
scheme, municipal legislative bodies may submit an ordinance to the 
city's voters providing for election of the body's members by or from 
a specified number of districts (Government Code Section 34871) and 
may amend or repeal the ordinance in the same manner (Section 34873). 
The statutory restriction is spelled out: 
No amendatory ordinance 
may be submitted to the voters if one or more entire legislative 
districts are eliminated orior to the termination of the term of office 
o~ the councilman of or f~om the district (Government Code Section 
34875(b)). 
In support of this requirement, the Code states that 
ordinances establishing districts may only be amended or repealed 
if the term of office of any council members is not affected 
(Government Code Section 34873). 
The statutory requirement that changes in district boundaries not be 
effected during any incumbent's term is mandatory. 
Local offices are 
'3" 
I 
I 
, , 


Ms. Julie SoRelle 
Page 2 
March 21, 1983 
subject to regulation by the state legislature as authorized by the 
constitution. 
Cal. Const. Art. XI sec. 2; See People v. Branvard 
(1865) 27 Cal. 470; In Re Bulger (1873) 45 Cal. 553. 
Therefore, 
local officers have no power to act in conflict with general law 
without legislative authority to do so being delegated to them. 
See Proulx v. Graves (1904) 143 Cal. 243, 246. 
The City of Clearlake has authority to, and did, enact an ordinance 
repealing districts. 
But such ordinance may not become effective 
as to any incumbent until the expiration of the incumbent's term. 
Therefore, the ordinance's language that city council members be 
elected at large "in future elections" can only be interpreted to 
refer to future elections occurring at the end of the incumbent's 
term. 
Since the incumbent's district boundaries are not repealed until the 
expiration of the term, any recall proceeding commenced during the 
term must necessarily be carried out by voters living within the 
boundaries of the district from which the incumbent was elected. 
The electoral jurisdiction of the officer sought to be recalled 
still 
es within the "old" boundaries during the incumbent's term 
for recall purposes. 
See Elections Code Section 27004. 
This conclusion based on the district selection statute is consistent 
with the law governing filling vacancies arising by resignation, 
death or otherwise. 
If a vacancy should arise in a seat for reasons 
other than recall, it would be filled based on the boundaries exist-
ing at the beginning of the term. 
Sloan v. Donoghue (1942) 20 Cal. 
2d 607. 
If the vacancy were to be filled by election (Government 
Code Section 36512, 36512.1), voters would be qualified to vote in 
the vacancy election who resided within the boundaries existing at 
the beginning of the term. 
The apparent public policy protects the right of voters, who have 
selected a representative, to retain an elected official accountable 
to them during the term for which the incumbent was elected. 
The 
conclusion above regarding general law city recall is consistent with 
this policy. 
The only exception to the policy is that the body which created the 
office may abolish or change it at any time (People v. Branvard, supra, 
27 Cal. 470) and incumbents take office subject to that restriction. 
Martello v. Superior Court (1927) 202 Cal. 400,408. 
The exception 
does not apply in the instant case because the authority authorizing 
the creation of the city and its governing board, the Legislature, has 
not amended the boundaries. 
While the Legislature has delegated power 


Ms. Julie SoRelle 
Page 3 
March 21, 1983 
to the city and its voters (Government Code Section 34870 - 34884) to 
amend boundaries, as it has authority to do (See Proulx v. Graves, 
supra, 143 Cal. 243, 246) the delegated authority and any concomitant 
restrictions must be observed. 
The conclusion regarding proceedings being based on "old" district 
boundaries also avoids a logical inconsistency encountered if a 
different conclusion is adopted which gives effect to the boundary 
changes immediately. 
The ordinance here in question approved at-large 
selection "in future elections". 
City councils have authority to 
fill recall-caused vacancies by appointment if voters authorize that 
method. 
Elections Code Section 27311. 
Vacancies arising for other 
reasons may be filled by appointment. 
Government Code Section 36512. 
By its terms, the ordinance adopted by Clearlake's voters in 1982 
would not apply to such appointments. 
No logical reason appears to 
justify different boundary lines being used to select a successor 
when the vacancy is filled by election rather than by appointment. 
Indeed, recall election voters would undoubtedly be confused upon 
being instructed that their vote to fill recall vacancies by election 
meant that a new incumbent would be accountable to a different set of 
voters than if they chose to fill the vacancy by appointment. 
The only language potentially suggesting that the change to an at-large 
constituency may not be effected immediately is found in Government 
Code Section 34878: 
"If a majority of the registered voters of the city, who vote, 
vote in favor of the ordinance, at the expiration of the terms 
of office of the members of the legislative body, or as 
rovided b ordinance, members of the legislative body shall 
be elected by or from} the districts described, or by or 
from districts with an elective mayor, and in the manner 
provided. II 
(Emphasis added). 
The emphasized language is ambiguous because it is not clear to what 
"ord i nance" it refers. 
However, the reference to "ordinance" is undoubtedly not a reference 
to the very ordinance repealing the districts. 
To interpret it

otherwise would render useless the prohibitions in Government Code 
Section 34873 and 34875 against affecting current terms of office, 
a conclusion to be avoided especially since the latter two Code 
sections apply specifically to amendatory ordinances repealing 
district lines, and the former ambiguous section refers to ordinances 
adopting district boundaries.

Ronald V. Thunen, Jr., City Attorney 
RE: 
Possible Recall of Councilman Achter 
Local newspapers have reported a possible attempt to 
recall Councilman Achter. 
You have asked whether Councilman 
Achter would be recalled by district or at large. 
Facts: 
When Clearlake was incorporated in November, 
1980, five councilmembers were elected at large. 
At that 
election, voters were also asked to vote on two 
propositions: B. 
Shall members of the city Council in 
future elections be elected by districts? 
C. 
Shall members 
of the City Council in future elections be elected at large? 
We are informed that proposition B passed, but that the 
vote on proposition C indicates that some voters probably 
voted "yes" to both measures: 
Accordingly, it was 
determined that at the 1982 municipal general election, two 
councilmen would be elected by district. 
Gov. Code Section 
34884. 
Councilman Achter, as one of the two members elected 
with the lowest vote, was one of the two original 
councilmembers whose term was scheduled to expire. 
The Council, by resolution No. 81-54 established 
councilmanic districts prior to the time for filing 
nomination papers for the 1982 municipal general election. 
At the same time, the Council, believing that the question 
of district versus at large election of councilmembers had 
been presented to the voters in a confusing manner in 1980 
(No pre-election publicity had been given to the issue, as 
attention had focused primarily on the question of 
incorporation itself, and secondarily on the council 
candidates themselves, to the exclusion of other issues.) 
passed resolution No. 81-53, placing the following 
proposition on the same ballot: 
"Shall members of the City Council be elected at large 
in future elections?" 
Councilman Achter filed for election from District No. 
4, ran unopposed, and was elected. 
The ballot proposition 
-1-


also passed by a 5 to 1 margin and in District 4, the 
proposition passed by a margin of 3 to 1, after receiving 
considerable pre-election publicity and discussion within 
the community. 
The issue thus presented by the current possible recall 
move is whether because Mr. Achter was elected by the voters 
of District 4, recall proceedings shall be restricted to 
registered voters in that district, or, because the voters 
of the whole city have expressed a preference for at large 
voting in all future elections, a recall should be conducted 
on an "at large" basis. 
I conclude that the will of the 
people as expressed by their vote in April 1982 should 
prevail, and that an "at large" recall would be the proper 
format. 
My analysis follows. 
ANALYSIS 
Normally, a councilman elected by district can only be 
recalled by district. 
El. C. Sec. 27211, Sec. 27004. 
In 
other words, the proponents, the signers of the petition, 
and voters at the recall election must 
all be registered 
voters in the councilmanic district. 
Moreover, if the 
decision is to recall and to re-fill the seat by a 
subsequent special election, that election would also be 
limited to the district. 
In this case, however, the voters have already rejected 
the district concept and strongly stated a preference for at 
large election. 
It would appear to frustrate the will of 
the people of the City to conduct any council election on a 
district basis after the 1982 election. 
Further, an anomaly 
currently exists in Clearlake, in that three councilmembers 
were elected at large, and two by districts, thereby giving 
the residents of districts two and four a disproportionate 
representation. * 
To hold Mr. Achter accountable at this 
time only to the voters of District 4 would be to perpetuate 
the second class status of the voters in former districts 1, 
3 and 5, as they will never have a representative of their 
own. 
Once the voters have elected to fill all future 
council vacancies by at large voting, the only fair approach 
to recall is to make all councilmembers accountable to the 
electorate at large immediately, rather than to wait for 
expiration of the term for which a councilman was elected by 
district. 
Any contrary result does violence to the 
cherished Constitutional concept of "one man, one vote" 
expressed in Baker v. Carr. 
In summary, I conclude that two well-established 
principles of American Constitutional law: (A) That the will 
of the people, as expressed at the polls, is to be 
effectuated insofar as is practicable; and (B) That the 
citizens of a community are entitled to equal voting power 
whenever practicable, dictate the conclusion that an "at 
-2-


large" recall format should be followed with respect to Mr. 
Achter's seat at this time. 
I am informed that you have received a tentative 
response from the Secretary of State's office indicating a 
contrary conclusion. 
I will submit this opinion to that 
office for their consideration. 
I do not believe that, if 
that office considers all of the matters discussed herein, 
they will disagree with my conclusion.

Census was conducted without regard to the boundaries or 
future needs of the City, as it did not exist then) the City 
Council attempted to divide the City into five districts by

numbers 
of housing units and numbers of registered voters. 
Essentially, then, a voter in former districts 1, 3, or 5 
can be said to possess 3/25,000 of the voting power in the 
City, but a resident in District 4 now possesses 28/25,000 
of the voting power. 
A district recall continues this 
disproportion until 1986; an "at large" recall, even if 
unsuccessful at any stage, would effectively convert Mr. 
Achter's present district constituency into a citywide 
constituency, by making him answerable to all of the people, 
not just those who elected him, thereby cancelling the 
disporportional advantage now possessed by residents of 
former District 4.

In view of the fact that considerable publicity has been given 
to the position of the Clearlake Citizens' Committee that one 
of the changes in municipal government which they hope to a-
chieve by means of the pending recall is redefinition of the 
job of the City Clerk and a corresponding increase in the sal-
ary for that position, I conclude that Government Code Section 
87100 
prohibits you from making any decision relating to the 
validity of the recall proceedings at any stage of the pro-
ceeding. 
Government Code Section 87103 defines financial interest as 
including any source of income of $250.00 or more which was 
promised within twelve months prior to the time when the de-
cision is to be made. 
The case of Witt vs. Morrow, 70 Cal. Ap. 
3rd 817 (1977) indicates that it is not necessary to prove that 
the decision of the official will have a significant impact on 
the financial interest, but rather only that it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect. 
It must be stated that it is now reasonably foresee-
able that a successful recall election will have a material 
effect on your compensation as City Clerk over the balance of 
your four year term. 
,I am mindful of the fact that Government Code Section 87101 
provides that Section 87100 does not apply where there is no 
one else to make the decision. 
However, it is also true that 
Elections Code Section 22003 permits the governing body of any 
city to request the Board of Supervisors of the county to per-
mit the County Clerk to render specified services to the city 
relating to the conduct of an election. 
I have contacted the 
County Clerk, who has stated that she is willing to perform 
the task of verifying signatures on the various recall peti-
tions.

I recently received a memo from the City Attorney advising 
me of his conclusion that prohibits me from making any de-
cision relating to the validity of the recall proceedings at 
any stage of the proceedings by virtue of Government Code 
Sections 87100 and 87103. 
Government Code Section 87100 provides that "no public 
official at any level of state or local government shall 
make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use 
his official position to influence a governmental decision 
in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial 
interest". 
Government Code Section 87103 provides that "an official has 
a financial interest in a decision ... if it is reasonabl~ 
forseeable that the decision will have a material financ~al 
effect ... on ... any source of income ... aggregating $250.00 
or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to 
the public official within 12 months prior to the time when 
the decision is made." 
The statement "reasonably forseeable" brings the questions: 
1) 
Will the recall proceed? 
2) 
Will the recall be successful and if so, how many council-
men will be recalled? 
3) 
Who will be successful in replacing the councilmen recalled, 
if the recall is successful? 
4) 
Will the new councilmen favor the statement in Mr. Thunen's 
memo which states, " ... . the position of the Clearlake 
Citizens' Committee that one of the changes in municipal 
government which they hope to achieve by means of the 
pending recall is a redefinition of the job of City Clerk 


Clearlake Observer 
March 30, 1983 
Page 2 
and a corresponding increase in the salary for that 
position .... "? 
5) 
Is Mr. Thunen correct in that statement and his view that 
"considerable publicity" has been given to that position? 
6) 
Will any resolution be passed during my term of office 
which will increase the salary of the City Clerk? 
Any mention of an increase in the salary of the City Clerk made 
by the Clearlake Citizens' Committee or publicized cannot be 
"a promise to a public official" since that body has no power 
to make such a decision. 
No material financial gain has been 
"provided to, received by, or promised to" me as City Clerk. 
I feel that this attempt to cast suspicion on my actions as 
they relate to the conduction of recall proceedings (a duty 
of the City Clerk by California State Code) is merely a tactic 
to delay the normal course of events which mayor may not result 
in the recall of one to four councilmen. 
Mr. Thunen also provided me with a memo stating his opinion that 
any recall affecting Councilmen Robey or Achter should be 
conducted on an "at large" basis. 
An opinion of the Elections 
Department of the Secretary of State's Office takes exactly the 
opposite view in stating they feel any recall affecting those 
Councilmen should be conducted "by district". 
Possibly the City Attorney and the City Council should look 
back on a possible conflict of interest regarding the interviews 
for City Attorney in which Mr. Thunen sat in on the interviews 
and advised the Council on the capabilities of the applicants. 
Those interviews resulted in a contract for Mr. Thunen at the 
rate of $1,500.00 per month plus a 6 month extension of the 
contract at that same rate of pay. 
Sincerely, 
Julie SoRelle 
City Clerk

sections.question: You are the City Clerk and you want to make and participate in the decisions related to this recall election. The City Attorney indicated that you should disqualify yourself from any involvement because one of the changes hoped to be achieved by the recall election is a redefinition of the position of City Clerk, with a corresponding increase in salary. You have asked whether your participation in the recall decisions will involve a conflict of interest.
sections.conclusion:
above regarding general law city recall is consistent with 
this policy. 
The only exception to the policy is that the body which created the 
office may abolish or change it at any time (People v. Branvard, supra, 
27 Cal. 470) and incumbents take office subject to that restriction. 
Martello v. Superior Court (1927) 202 Cal. 400,408. 
The exception 
does not apply in the instant case because the authority authorizing 
the creation of the city and its governing board, the Legislature, has 
not amended the boundaries. 
While the Legislature has delegated power 


Ms. Julie SoRelle  
March 21, 1983 
to the city and its voters (Government Code Section 34870 - 34884) to 
amend boundaries, as it has authority to do (See Proulx v. Graves, 
supra, 143 Cal. 243, 246) the delegated authority and any concomitant 
restrictions must be observed. 
The conclusion regarding proceedings being based on "old" district 
boundaries also avoids a logical inconsistency encountered if a 
different conclusion is adopted which gives effect to the boundary 
changes immediately. 
The ordinance here in question approved at-large 
selection "in future elections". 
City councils have authority to 
fill recall-caused vacancies by appointment if voters authorize that 
method. 
Elections Code Section 27311. 
Vacancies arising for other 
reasons may be filled by appointment. 
Government Code Section 36512. 
By its terms, the ordinance adopted by Clearlake's voters in 1982 
would not apply to such appointments. 
No logical reason appears to 
justify different boundary lines being used to select a successor 
when the vacancy is filled by election rather than by appointment. 
Indeed, recall election voters would undoubtedly be confused upon 
being instructed that their vote to fill recall vacancies by election 
meant that a new incumbent would be accountable to a different set of 
voters than if they chose to fill the vacancy by appointment. 
The only language potentially suggesting that the change to an at-large 
constituency may not be effected immediately is found in Government 
Code Section 34878: 
"If a majority of the registered voters of the city, who vote, 
vote in favor of the ordinance, at the expiration of the terms 
of office of the members of the legislative body, or as 
rovided b ordinance, members of the legislative body shall 
be elected by or from} the districts described, or by or 
from districts with an elective mayor, and in the manner 
provided. II 
(Emphasis added). 
The emphasized language is ambiguous because it is not clear to what 
"ord i nance" it refers. 
However, the reference to "ordinance" is undoubtedly not a reference 
to the very ordinance repealing the districts. 
To interpret it 


Ms. Julie SoRelle  
March 21, 1983 
otherwise would render useless the prohibitions in Government Code 
Section 34873 and 34875 against affecting current terms of office, 
a conclusion to be avoided especially since the latter two Code 
sections apply specifically to amendatory ordinances repealing 
district lines, and the former ambiguous section refers to ordinances 
adopting district boundaries.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a City Clerk can participate in recall election decisions when the recall campaign seeks to redefine the City Clerk position and increase its salary, which could materially affect the Clerk's financial interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that Government Code Section 82030(b)(2) excludes salary from local government agencies from the definition of 'income,' so the City Clerk may participate in recall election decisions despite potential effects on her salary.

QUESTION: You are the City Clerk and you want to make and participate in the decisions related to this recall election. The City Attorney indicated that you should disqualify yourself from any involvement because one of the changes hoped to be achieved by the recall election is a redefinition of the position of City Clerk, with a corresponding increase in salary. You have asked whether your participation in the recall decisions will involve a conflict of interest.

CONCLUSION: above regarding general law city recall is consistent with 
this policy. 
The only exception to the policy is that the body which created the 
office may abolish or change it at any time (People v. Branvard, supra, 
27 Cal. 470) and incumbents take office subject to that restriction. 
Martello v. Superior Court (1927) 202 Cal. 400,408. 
The exception 
does not apply in the instant case because the authority authorizing 
the creation of the city and its governing board, the Legislature, has 
not amended the boundaries. 
While the Legislature has delegated power 


Ms. Julie SoRelle  
March 21, 1983 
to the city and its voters (Government Code Section 34870 - 34884) to 
amend boundaries, as it has authority to do (See Proulx v. Graves, 
supra, 143 Cal. 243, 246) the delegated authority and any concomitant 
restrictions must be observed. 
The conclusion regarding proceedings being based on "old" district 
boundaries also avoids a logical inconsistency encountered if a 
different conclusion is adopted which gives effect to the boundary 
changes immediately. 
The ordinance here in question approved at-large 
selection "in future elections". 
City councils have authority to 
fill recall-caused vacancies by appointment if voters authorize that 
method. 
Elections Code Section 27311. 
Vacancies arising for other 
reasons may be filled by appointment. 
Government Code Section 36512. 
By its terms, the ordinance adopted by Clearlake's voters in 1982 
would not apply to such appointments. 
No logical reason appears to 
justify different boundary lines being used to select a successor 
when the vacancy is filled by election rather than by appointment. 
Indeed, recall election voters would undoubtedly be confused upon 
being instructed that their vote to fill recall vacancies by election 
meant that a new incumbent would be accountable to a different set of 
voters than if they chose to fill the vacancy by appointment. 
The only language potentially suggesting that the change to an at-large 
constituency may not be effected immediately is found in Government 
Code Section 34878: 
"If a majority of the registered voters of the city, who vote, 
vote in favor of the ordinance, at the expiration of the terms 
of office of the members of the legislative body, or as 
rovided b ordinance, members of the legislative body shall 
be elected by or from} the districts described, or by or 
from districts with an elective mayor, and in the manner 
provided. II 
(Emphasis added). 
The emphasized language is ambiguous because it is not clear to what 
"ord i nance" it refers. 
However, the reference to "ordinance" is undoubtedly not a reference 
to the very ordinance repealing the districts. 
To interpret it 


Ms. Julie SoRelle  
March 21, 1983 
otherwise would render useless the prohibitions in Government Code 
Section 34873 and 34875 against affecting current terms of office, 
a conclusion to be avoided especially since the latter two Code 
sections apply specifically to amendatory ordinances repealing 
district lines, and the former ambiguous section refers to ordinances 
adopting district boundaries.

embedding.summary: FPPC Advice Letter A-83-106 addresses whether City Clerk Julie SoRelle has a conflict of interest participating in recall election decisions when the recall campaign seeks to redefine her position and increase her salary.
